stem: A hospital uses a rapid antibody test to screen for HIV. The test has a sensitivity of 92% and a specificity of 89%. The prevalence of HIV in the patient population is estimated to be between 2.7% and 4.5%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has HIV? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(HIV | positive test) ranges from 0.188 to 0.283 as prevalence varies from 2.7% to 4.5%. Point estimate at 3.6% prevalence: 0.238.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.11.
The confidence interval [0.188, 0.283] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (92%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
